subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 10! In this unit you will become confident and accurate at multiplying and dividing large numbers. You already know your times tables and how to multiply small numbers. Now you will learn efficient written methods that let you handle numbers in the thousands, multiply by two-digit numbers, carry out long division, and even multiply and divide decimals. You will also learn how the order of operations decides which calculation to do first when several are written together.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 6 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Multiply numbers up to four digits by a single-digit number using the standard written method.
Multiply by multiples of 10 (such as 20, 30, 700) using place-value patterns.
Multiply a three-digit or four-digit number by a two-digit number (long multiplication).
Use the grid method as a second way to multiply and to check your work.
Carry out long division of large numbers, expressing remainders correctly.
Multiply and divide decimals by whole numbers.
Apply the correct order of operations (brackets, then × and ÷, then + and −).
Estimate products and quotients by rounding to check that answers are reasonable.
Solve multi-step word problems and check answers using inverse operations.

concepts.title: Multiplying Large Numbers by a Single Digit
concepts.explanation: When you multiply a large number by a single digit, you use the standard written method (sometimes called the standard algorithm). You multiply one digit at a time, working from right to left, and you carry whenever a product is bigger than 9. The whole method rests on place value: the ones, tens, hundreds and thousands must each stay in their own column. Let us work through 1347 × 8 carefully, one column at a time.
concepts.example: Step 1 – Estimate first. Round 1347 to 1000: 1000 × 8 = 8000. Round it up to 1500: 1500 × 8 = 12,000. So a correct answer must lie between 8000 and 12,000. Keep that range in mind.

concepts.title: Multiplying by Multiples of 10, 100 and 1000
concepts.explanation: Multiplying by 10, 100 or 1000 follows a beautiful place-value pattern. When you multiply by 10, every digit moves one place to the left and a 0 fills the empty ones column: 23 × 10 = 230. Multiplying by 100 moves every digit two places left: 23 × 100 = 2300. Multiplying by 1000 moves them three places: 23 × 1000 = 23,000. It is tempting to say “just add a zero”, but a careful mathematician thinks about why it works: the digits are sliding into bigger place-value columns, and the zero is a place holder that keeps…
concepts.example: To multiply by other multiples of 10 – like 20, 30 or 700 – you split the multiple. For example, 20 = 2 × 10, so multiply by 2 and then by 10:

concepts.title: Long Multiplication (Two-Digit Multipliers)
concepts.explanation: When both numbers have two or more digits, you multiply in parts and then add the parts together. Each part is called a partial product. There are two popular methods, and it is worth knowing both: the grid method shows clearly what is happening, and the column method is quicker once you understand it. The grid method breaks each number into its place-value parts. For 23 × 47, split 23 into 20 + 3 and 47 into 40 + 7, then fill a grid:
concepts.example: Now add all four partial products: 800 + 140 + 120 + 21 = 1081. Every part is visible, so it is easy to see nothing has been missed.

concepts.title: Long Division
concepts.explanation: Let us divide 4356 ÷ 12. First estimate: 12 × 300 = 3600 and 12 × 400 = 4800, so the answer is between 300 and 400. Divide: 12 does not go into 4, so look at 43. 43 ÷ 12 = 3 (because 3 × 12 = 36). Write 3 above the 3 of 43.

concepts.title: Multiplying and Dividing Decimals
concepts.explanation: Money and measurements are full of decimals, so you must be able to multiply and divide them by whole numbers. The good news is that you use exactly the same written methods – you simply keep track of the decimal point. To multiply a decimal by a whole number, ignore the decimal point at first, multiply as usual, then put the point back so the answer has the same number of decimal places as the decimal you started with. For example, 3.6 × 7: work out 36 × 7 = 252, then – because 3.6 has one decimal place – the…
concepts.example: A real example: a litre of camel milk costs 3.6 dollars at the Hargeisa market (we will use dollars for a tidy decimal). Seven litres cost 3.6 × 7 = $25.20. Notice we write money to two decimal places, so 25.2 becomes…

concepts.title: Order of Operations
concepts.explanation: What is the value of 6 + 3 × 4? If you work from left to right you might say 9 × 4 = 36, but that is wrong. Mathematicians all over the world agree to do multiplication and division before addition and subtraction, so 3 × 4 = 12 is done first, then 6 + 12 = 18. Without an agreed order, the same calculation would give different answers to different people – so the order of operations is a shared rule that keeps everyone in step. I – Indices (powers), such as squaring.
concepts.example: D and M – Division and Multiplication, worked left to right.

methods.title: Long multiplication (3-digit × 2-digit)
steps: Estimate first by rounding both numbers.
Multiply by the ones digit to get the first partial product.
Write a 0 in the ones column, then multiply by the tens digit for the second partial product.
Add the two partial products.
Compare with your estimate to check the size.

methods.title: Long division
steps: Estimate the quotient by rounding.
Divide the divisor into the digits from the left.
Multiply, then subtract, then bring down the next digit.
Repeat divide, multiply, subtract, bring down until no digits remain.
Write any remainder, then check by multiplying the quotient by the divisor.

methods.title: Multiplying or dividing a decimal by a whole number
steps: Ignore the decimal point and calculate with whole numbers.
Count the decimal places in the original decimal.
Put the point back so the answer has the same number of decimal places.
For division, keep the point in the answer directly above the point in the dividend.

methods.title: Long division, exact
steps: Solution: 16 into 14 will not go, so use 145.
Bring down 6 to make 16.
16 ÷ 16 = 1 exactly.

methods.title: Long division with a remainder
steps: Solution: 34 into 36 goes once (34), remainder 2.
Bring down 7 to make 27; 34 into 27 will not go, so write 0 and bring down 8 to make 278.
Quotient 108 remainder 6.

methods.title: Multiplying a decimal
methods.example: A litre of oil costs 4.5 dollars. What do 8 litres cost?
steps: Solution: Ignore the point: 45 × 8 = 360.
There is one decimal place in 4.5, so the answer has one decimal place: 36.0, i.e.
Estimate 4 × 8 = 32 confirms the size.

workedExamples.title: Single-digit multiplication
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Estimate 2500 × 7 = 17,500. Ones: 6×7 = 42, write 2 carry 4. Tens: 5×7 = 35, +4 = 39, write 9 carry 3. Hundreds: 4×7 = 28, +3 = 31, write 1 carry 3. Thousands: 2×7 = 14, +3 = 17, write 17. Answer 17,192, close to the estimate. ✓

workedExamples.title: Multiplying by a multiple of 10
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 1234 × 3 = 3702, then × 10 gives 37,020. Estimate 1000 × 30 = 30,000, so 37,020 is a sensible size. ✓

workedExamples.title: Long multiplication
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 234 × 6 = 1404 (first partial product). 234 × 50 = 11,700 (second partial product, ending in 0). Add: 1404 + 11,700 = 13,104. Estimate 200 × 60 = 12,000, so 13,104 is reasonable. ✓

workedExamples.title: Long division, exact
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 16 into 14 will not go, so use 145. 145 ÷ 16 = 9 (9×16 = 144), remainder 1. Bring down 6 to make 16. 16 ÷ 16 = 1 exactly. Quotient 91. Check: 91 × 16 = 1456. ✓

workedExamples.title: Long division with a remainder
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 34 into 36 goes once (34), remainder 2. Bring down 7 to make 27; 34 into 27 will not go, so write 0 and bring down 8 to make 278. 278 ÷ 34 = 8 (8×34 = 272), remainder 6. Quotient 108 remainder 6. Check: 34 × 108 = 3672, and 3672 + 6 = 3678. ✓

workedExamples.title: Multiplying a decimal
workedExamples.prompt: A litre of oil costs 4.5 dollars. What do 8 litres cost?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Ignore the point: 45 × 8 = 360. There is one decimal place in 4.5, so the answer has one decimal place: 36.0, i.e. $36.00. Estimate 4 × 8 = 32 confirms the size. ✓

workedExamples.title: Order of operations
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Brackets first: 8 − 3 = 5. Then multiply: 6 × 5 = 30. Then add: 5 + 30 = 35. ✓

workedExamples.title: A two-step problem
workedExamples.prompt: Yusuf buys 24 boxes of dates with 36 dates in each box. He shares them equally among 8 families. How many dates does each family receive?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: First multiply: 24 × 36 = 864 dates in total. Then divide: 864 ÷ 8 = 108 dates per family. Check: 108 × 8 = 864. ✓

workedExamples.title: Multiply the decimals
workedExamples.prompt: Multiply the decimals: a) 3.6 × 7 b) 4.5 × 8 c) 2.4 × 6.

workedExamples.title: Divide the decimals
workedExamples.prompt: Divide the decimals: a) 25.2 ÷ 7 b) 8.4 × ... no, 8.4 ÷ 4 c) 14.4 ÷ 6.

workedExamples.title: Order of operations
workedExamples.prompt: Order of operations: a) 20 − 12 ÷ 4 b) 5 + 6 × (8 − 3) c) 30 ÷ 5 + 2 × 3.

workedExamples.title: Calculate 678 × 94, then verify with an estimate
workedExamples.prompt: Calculate 678 × 94, then verify with an estimate.

activities.title: Market Trader Challenge
activities.materials: paper, pencil, and small stones or bottle caps as ‘shillings’.
steps: Set up a pretend Hargeisa market stall with five goods and a price for each (for example, mangoes at 45 shillings, a bag of rice at 234 shillings).
A customer buys several of one item – say 30 mangoes. Work out the cost using multiplication by a multiple of 10.
Now the customer pays with a large note. Use division to work out the change.
Swap roles and repeat. Keep a written record of every calculation and check each one with an estimate.
This activity turns the whole unit into a game and shows how traders use multiplication and division every single day.

activities.title: Heartbeat Investigation
activities.materials: a clock or timer, and paper.
steps: Measure your resting heart rate: count your pulse for one minute.
Multiply by 60 to find beats per hour, then by 24 to find beats per day.
Multiply by 365 to estimate beats in a year – a huge number! Estimate first, then calculate.
Compare results with family members. Whose heart beats most in a day? Show your working.

activities.title: Grid-Method Art
activities.materials: squared paper, a ruler and coloured pencils.
steps: Choose a long-multiplication question such as 156 × 43.
Draw a large grid and split each number into place-value parts (100 + 50 + 6 and 40 + 3).
Fill each box with its partial product and colour boxes of equal value the same colour.
Add the partial products, then check your total using the column method. Do the two answers match?

activities.title: Long-Division Relay (Family or Group Game)
activities.materials: two or more players, paper and pencils.
steps: One player writes a long-division question, such as 3678 ÷ 34.
Players take turns to do ONE step each of the cycle: divide, multiply, subtract, bring down.
If a player makes a mistake, the next player must spot and fix it before carrying on.
Finish by multiplying the quotient back to check. The team wins if the check matches!

activities.title: BIDMAS Puzzle Maker
activities.materials: paper, pencil, and the digits 2, 3, 4 and 5.
steps: Using the four digits once each, with any operations and brackets, try to make the number 20 exactly.
Write down your calculation and check it obeys the order of operations.
Now try to make 14, then 30. How many different targets can you hit?
Give a friend one of your puzzles and see if they can find the same answer.

activities.title: Real-World Estimation Hunt
activities.materials: a notebook and your everyday surroundings.
steps: Find three real quantities to estimate: for example, how many litres of water your family uses in a week, or how many dates are in a full box.
Write a sensible estimate for each using rounding.
Where you can, find the exact number and compare it with your estimate.
Reflect: which estimates were closest, and how could you estimate better next time?

After your activities, talk or write about these questions: Which method of multiplication do you find easiest, and why? What is the trickiest part of long division for you? Where did you see multiplication and division in real life this week? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session, and set yourself one thing to practise more.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: What is the value of 6 + 3 × 4? If you work from left to right you might say 9 × 4 = 36, but that is wrong. Mathematicians all over the world agree to do multiplication and division before addition and subtraction, so 3 × 4 = 12 is done first, then 6 + 12 = 18. Without an agreed order, the same calculation would give different answers to different people – so the order of operations is a shared rule that keeps everyone in step.

rules.title: Key idea 2
rules.text: that division and multiplication share the same rank, so you work them left to right, not division-before-multiplication. The same is true for addition and subtraction. Getting this right matters more and more as you move towards algebra, where the order of operations is used in every single line.

terms: The number divided / the number you divide by
What is left over after dividing
One part of a long multiplication
A sensible approximate answer used to check
commonMistakes: Leaving the 0 off the second line
Write the 0 in the ones column first
Write carries small above the next column
Increase that digit and subtract again

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Calculate 678 × 94, then verify with an estimate.
Calculate 4356 ÷ 12 and prove your answer by multiplying back.
Find the error: someone writes 456 × 23 as 1368 + 912 = 2280. Explain the mistake and give the correct answer.
Fill in the missing digit so the calculation is correct: 3 _ 4 × 6 = 2004. What is the missing digit?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The second partial product 456 × 20 should be 9120, not 912 – the zero on the second line was left off. Correct: 1368 + 9120 = 10,488.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Work out 100 − (4 + 6) × 8 ÷ 4, showing each step of the order of operations.
Explain the key idea in Order of Operations.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: What is the value of 6 + 3 × 4? If you work from left to right you might say 9 × 4 = 36, but that is wrong. Mathematicians all over the world agree to do multiplication and division before addition and subtraction, so 3 × 4 = 12 is done first, then 6 + 12 = 18. Without an agreed order, the same calculation would give different answers to different people – so the order of operations is a shared rule that keeps everyone in step. I – Indices (powers), such as squaring.

questions.question: Calculate 2456 × 7.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 10 methods.
questions.explanation: Estimate 2500 × 7 = 17,500. Ones: 6×7 = 42, write 2 carry 4. Tens: 5×7 = 35, +4 = 39, write 9 carry 3. Hundreds: 4×7 = 28, +3 = 31, write 1 carry 3. Thousands: 2×7 = 14, +3 = 17, write 17. Answer 17,192, close to the estimate. ✓

questions.question: Calculate 1234 × 30.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 10 methods.
questions.explanation: 1234 × 3 = 3702, then × 10 gives 37,020. Estimate 1000 × 30 = 30,000, so 37,020 is a sensible size. ✓

questions.question: Calculate 234 × 56.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 10 methods.
questions.explanation: 234 × 6 = 1404 (first partial product). 234 × 50 = 11,700 (second partial product, ending in 0). Add: 1404 + 11,700 = 13,104. Estimate 200 × 60 = 12,000, so 13,104 is reasonable. ✓

questions.question: Calculate 1456 ÷ 16.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 10 methods.
questions.explanation: 16 into 14 will not go, so use 145. 145 ÷ 16 = 9 (9×16 = 144), remainder 1. Bring down 6 to make 16. 16 ÷ 16 = 1 exactly. Quotient 91. Check: 91 × 16 = 1456. ✓

rounds.explanation: 16 into 14 will not go, so use 145. 145 ÷ 16 = 9 (9×16 = 144), remainder 1. Bring down 6 to make 16. 16 ÷ 16 = 1 exactly. Quotient 91. Check: 91 × 16 = 1456. ✓

rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 10 methods.
rounds.explanation: Estimate 2500 × 7 = 17,500. Ones: 6×7 = 42, write 2 carry 4. Tens: 5×7 = 35, +4 = 39, write 9 carry 3. Hundreds: 4×7 = 28, +3 = 31, write 1 carry 3. Thousands: 2×7 = 14, +3 = 17, write 17. Answer 17,192, close to the estimate. ✓

rounds.explanation: 234 × 6 = 1404 (first partial product). 234 × 50 = 11,700 (second partial product, ending in 0). Add: 1404 + 11,700 = 13,104. Estimate 200 × 60 = 12,000, so 13,104 is reasonable. ✓

selfAssessment: I can multiply numbers up to four digits by a single-digit number using the standard written method.
I can multiply by multiples of 10 (such as 20, 30, 700) using place-value patterns.
I can multiply a three-digit or four-digit number by a two-digit number (long multiplication).
I can use the grid method as a second way to multiply and to check your work.
I can carry out long division of large numbers, expressing remainders correctly.
I can multiply and divide decimals by whole numbers.
I can apply the correct order of operations (brackets, then × and ÷, then + and −).
I can estimate products and quotients by rounding to check that answers are reasonable.
